title: Travis McKenna
text:
CV | PhilPeople | Google Scholar | tsmckenn@ncsu.edu

I am an Assistant Professor of Philosophy at North Carolina State University.
I work primarily in the philosophy of science, though I am also interested in the philosophy of language. I’m generally interested in questions to do with models and explanation, how applied mathematics works, the role that laws play in scientific inquiry, multiscalar modelling strategies of various kinds, and conceptual engineering.
For the 2024-25 academic year, I was the Ahmanson Postdoctoral Instructor in Philosophy of Science at the California Institute of Technology. Before that, I completed my PhD in Philosophy at the University of Pittsburgh, advised by Bob Batterman and Mark Wilson.
Before arriving in Pittsburgh in 2018, I completed a BSci (Hons) in Mathematics and Philosophy at the University of Sydney, where I worked with David Macarthur. Way before that, I completed a BA (Hons) in Ancient History and Modern Languages, also at the University of Sydney.
Outside of philosophy, I like learning languages, writing short stories, and playing chess. Before I moved to Pittsburgh I played and coached cricket for the mighty North Sydney Bears.

title: Travis McKenna
text:
Here’s a list of courses I’ve taught at various places I’ve been, along with some syllabi, slides, and handouts.

NC State


PHI 340: Philosophy of Science, Spring 2026.


Description · Syllabus


A general introduction to the philosophy of science. We start by thinking about the classic problem of demarcating science from pseudoscience. Having cut our teeth a bit, we then move on to questions about induction, confirmation, observation, and theory change. This puts us in a position to think carefully about the important question of scientific realism: do scientific theories provide us with a true description of reality?




PHI 330: Metaphysics, Spring 2026.


Description · Syllabus


A general introduction to metaphysics. We begin with a sort of logic boot camp, after which we will be equipped to think about various ontological questions concerning constitution, personal identity, abstracta, and so on. We then move on to thinking about the nature of possibility and time, before closing by working through closely related questions about causation and laws of nature.




PHI 340: Philosophy of Science, Fall 2025.


Description · Syllabus


A general introduction to the philosophy of science. We start by thinking about the classic problem of demarcating science from pseudoscience. Having cut our teeth a bit, we then move on to questions about induction, confirmation, observation, and theory change. This puts us in a position to think carefully about the important question of scientific realism: do scientific theories provide us with a true description of reality?




Caltech


HUM/PI 41: Knowledge and Reality, Spring 2025.


Description · Syllabus · Handout: Gettier Cases · Handout: Time


An introduction to metaphysics and epistemology. We start with epistemology, considering various classic analyses of knowledge and the problems by which they are beset. We then consider questions about skepticism and the kinds of justification we can have for our beliefs. Moving onto metaphysics, we work our way through a host of classic debates in metaphysics: personal identity, time, causation, laws of nature, and so on.




HPS/PI 120: Introduction to Philosophy of Science, Fall 2024.


Description · Syllabus · Handout: Grue · Handout: The Pessimistic Metainduction


A general introduction to the philosophy of science. We start by thinking about the classic problem of demarcating science from pseudoscience. Having cut our teeth a bit, we then move on to questions about induction, confirmation, observation, and theory change. This puts us in a position to think carefully about the important question of scientific realism: do scientific theories provide us with a true description of reality?




Pittsburgh


PHIL0360: Introduction to Biomedical Ethics, Fall 2023.


Description · Syllabus


We begin with a quick whirlwind tour through deontological and consequentialist moral theories. We then think about various bioethical questions that arise in the context of both the ends and beginnings of our lives. We close by examining various thorny ethical challenges that arise from the use of technology in different biomedical contexts.




PHIL0300: Introduction to Ethics, Summer 2022.


Description · Syllabus


An introduction to moral philosophy by way of the two most prominent forms of moral theory: deontology and consequentialism. We begin by learning what these two kinds of moral theory are and how they differ, move on to thinking about the various arguments that have been offered in support of various forms of these theories, and end by considering how these theories help us to think about various applied ethical cases.

title: Travis McKenna
text:
I work in philosophy of science and metaphysics, especially laws of nature and scientific methodology.

Publications


Laws of Nature and their Supporting Casts, The British Journal for the Philosophy of Science, Forthcoming.


Abstract · Preprint


It is an underappreciated fact about scientific practice that laws are very often not capable of doing very much on their very own. Rather, in providing us with predictions and explanations they rely on a supporting cast of helpers – things like boundary conditions, material parameters, rigidity constraints, and so on. I argue that this fact spells serious trouble for Pragmatic Humean accounts that attempt to pick out laws by way of the explanatory or predictive roles they play in scientific practice.




Structure and Applied Mathematics, Synthese, 2022.


Abstract · Preprint


Mapping accounts hold that the application of mathematics to real world systems involves setting up the right kind of mapping between mathematical and physical structures. I argue that this picture relies on a strong assumption: that the mathematics involved in any application can be neatly packaged into a single mathematical structure. Looking at the multiscale modelling of sea ice, I suggest that this assumption is often too simple – real applications often require stitching together very different bits of mathematics in ways that resist this kind of tidy representation.




Lange on Minimal Model Explanations: A Defense of Batterman and Rice, Philosophy of Science, 2021.


Abstract · Preprint


Minimal model explanations are supposed to capture patterns of behaviour that show up across very different kinds of physical systems. Marc Lange has argued that this picture faces serious problems. I suggest that these objections miss their mark: they rest on treating minimal models as if they were aimed at explaining particular systems, rather than the robust patterns that cut across them.




Papers in Progress

 The following are at various stages of ‘in progress’. Please feel free to email me if you’d like to see a draft. Comments are more than welcome! 



What is an Instance of a Law of Nature?


Abstract


Philosophers commonly treat the relationship between scientific laws and the systems to which they apply on the model of that between a generalisation and its instances. I argue that this kind of idealised approach cannot sustain the kind of fine-grained, practice-sensitive picture that many contemporary philosophers hope for. The upshot is simple: if we want our theories of laws to shed light on the detailed workings of scientific practice, we must move beyond this idealisation.




Naturalistic Metaphysics and the Methodology of Science


Abstract


Nina Emery has recently argued that anyone who believes that metaphysical theories should not conflict with our best scientific theories should also think that metaphysicians should actively employ the methodology of contemporary science. That is, anyone who is a content naturalist must be a methodological naturalist. I explain why this argument does not work and hint at what this might teach us about the relationship between metaphysical and scientific theorising.




The Coordinating Role of Laws in Empirical Science


Abstract


In thinking about the role that laws play in scientific practice, most philosophers take for granted that laws should be treated as some kind or other of generalisation. I argue that this logic-inflected picture prevents us from noticing and making sense of some important and interesting features of the way that laws function in scientific practice. In its stead, I develop a picture that focusses on the role that laws play within the construction of scientific models.




Minimalism about Laws (with Tyler Hildebrand)


Abstract


Arguments based on scientific practice have become extremely common in the literature on laws of nature. It is not always clear, however, exactly how or why such arguments are supposed to work. We attempt to lay out some criteria for the success of such arguments and suggest that they are harder to satisfy than many may have realised. This leads us to a view that we call Minimalism.




Fundamental Laws and the Methodology of Science (with Gabrielle Kerbel)


Abstract


Metaphysicians often distinguish between fundamental and non-fundamental laws. In doing so, they often assume that such a distinction plays an important role in the methodology of science. We show that the notion of fundamental law that appears in scientific practice is highly non-uniform and messy. If metaphysicians want to make use of such a distinction, they must do more than simply gesture at the presence of such a distinction in the methodology of science.
